algorithmic + implementation | O(M log M) time, O(M) space, where M is the total number of Lunlun numbers up to a fixed maximum digit length (here depth=10)->O(k) time, O(k) space, where k is the requested index of the Lunlun number | The need to increase the maximum allowed length (depth) to support larger k would cause the DFS search space to grow roughly like 3^depth, suggesting poor scalability. | Profiling or simple estimate shows that most generated numbers are never used when k is small relative to M, indicating wasted work. | Global sort over all generated candidates dominates run time in micro-benchmarks, while only one order statistic (k-th) is required. | Constraints or editorial hints for the original problem (e.g., k up to 1e5) imply that a linear or near-linear-in-k solution is expected. | Recursion, although shallow here, becomes an obvious non-starter if the maximum allowed digit length had to grow significantly. | Replace recursive DFS generation of all numbers up to a fixed depth with an iterative BFS queue seeded with digits 1–9. | At each BFS step, pop the smallest unprocessed Lunlun number, decrement the remaining count, and stop as soon as the k-th number is reached instead of generating everything. | Generate successors by examining only the last digit and appending last_digit + {−1, 0, +1} within [0, 9], pushing valid numbers back to the queue. | Remove the global list accumulation and subsequent sort; the BFS order is inherently non-decreasing by construction. | Avoid eval(input()) in favor of a simple integer read helper, and centralize I/O via sys.stdin.readline for cheaper input handling. | Optionally, remove unused heavyweight imports in the fast template to minimize startup overhead, though this is a minor impact compared to the algorithm change. | algorithmic_overgeneration | global_sort | recursion_overhead | rigid_depth_cap | unsafe_input_eval | number generation | digit constraints | BFS | DFS | constructive enumeration | -like
